one_line_takeaway: A confidence-matrix abstraction turns diverse RTBF requests in vertical federated learning into a single closed-form update, yielding the first VFU framework that is simultaneously multi-target, asynchronous, and (for convex losses) certified — matching SOTA fidelity while adding capabilities no prior VFU method has.
key_finding: For synchronous client removal the method matches the state-of-the-art VFUFR baseline and beats VFULR on fidelity while retaining a large efficiency edge over retraining, AND it additionally delivers capabilities the baselines lack: feature/sensitive-info removal, asynchronous operation, and certified guarantees. Asynchronous unlearning with ~3/4 of clients online reaches essentially the same fidelity as the fully-synchronous system while cutting online time and communication cost by ~25%. Fidelity degrades noticeably only on low-dimensional datasets (Diabetes, 8 features), where removing a client/feature removes a larger share of information.
core_contribution: The paper presents the first Vertical Federated Unlearning (VFU) framework that (1) unifies multiple unlearning targets (client, feature, sensitive-information, sample, and class removal) through a single mechanism, (2) provides certified (approximate) unlearning guarantees for strongly-convex losses, and (3) introduces the first ASYNCHRONOUS VFU method so that only the requesting client and the active party must be online during forgetting. The unifying trick is a 'confidence matrix' maintained by the active party (in the AggVFL setting): each client's local model is treated as a feature extractor emitting class-confidence scores, and any unlearning target is expressed as a closed-form subtraction/update to this matrix, followed by fine-tuning that back-propagates from the stored matrix rather than raw data. Prior VFU methods (VFULR [8], VFUFR [26], GBDT-based [17], label-forgetting [12]) each handle only one removal type and/or one model family; Table 1 positions this work as the only one that is simultaneously multi-target, asynchronous, certified, and model-agnostic. These distinctions are the paper's OWN claims, not attributes of the cited baselines.
threat_model: This is a privacy-compliance / certified-unlearning paper, not an attack paper. The motivating requirement is the GDPR/CCPA 'right to be forgotten' (RTBF): after a user revokes consent, their data's influence must be provably removed from a trained VFL model. The security guarantee is an indistinguishability notion (Definitions 1-2): an (epsilon, delta)-certified unlearning method U produces a model that is statistically indistinguishable (bounded likelihood ratio) from a model retrained from scratch on the reduced dataset, so an adversary cannot tell whether the data was genuinely deleted or merely 'patched over.' A secondary privacy property the authors argue: the unlearning protocol transmits only confidence-vector differences and gradients — the SAME information already exchanged during normal AggVFL training — so it introduces no additional privacy-leakage surface, and raw features never leave each party's local domain. Certification is achieved by (a) injecting Gaussian noise b ~ N(0, sigma^2 I) into gradients during training/unlearning to bound parameter sensitivity, and (b) a first-round simultaneous gradient descent on new data plus gradient ascent on the removed data.
limitations: Stated by the authors: (1) the certified-unlearning theory applies ONLY to models with strongly-convex loss functions (e.g., MSE, L2-regularized logistic loss) — deep/non-convex models get the mechanism but not the formal guarantee; (2) the framework is confined to the AggVFL structure and does not yet extend to trainable-global-module Split-NN VFL. Reviewer-noted: the asynchronous contribution-factor stability rests on a first-order Taylor approximation that is only argued to be 'negligible' for shallow MLPs (full proof in appendix, not in the main text read); the full certification proof is in Appendix 5 and not verified here; feature/sensitive-info experiments are shown on only 2 datasets each due to page limits and are benchmarked only against Retrain (no unlearning baseline supports those scenarios).

datasets_used: Adult Income (48842 samples, 108 features, 16 clients, 27 features in removed client; tabular)
Credit (30000 samples, 23 features, 4 clients, 6 features in removed client; tabular; contains sensitive 'age' attribute)
Diabetes (768 samples, 8 features, 4 clients, 2 features in removed client; tabular; contains 'number of pregnancies' attribute)
Nursery (10950 samples, 19 features, 4 clients, 7 features in removed client; tabular)
Malware (49226 samples, 2081 features, 16 clients, 520 features in removed client; text/bag-of-words)
quantitative_results: LR synchronous client removal, average over 5 datasets (Accuracy/AUC): VFULR 0.791/0.814, VFUFR 0.806/0.837, Ours 0.806/0.836, Retrain (upper bound) 0.811/0.847 — Ours ties VFUFR and beats VFULR (Table 3).
MLP synchronous client removal, average (Accuracy/AUC): VFUFR 0.795/0.846, Ours 0.798/0.845, Retrain 0.827/0.870 (Table 4).
Low-dimensional Diabetes shows the largest gap: MLP client-removal accuracy Ours 0.636 vs Retrain 0.740; LR Diabetes AUC Ours 0.740 vs Retrain 0.782.

Efficiency (LR, Table 5): Ours and VFUFR use 50 epochs (early-stopped) vs Retrain's 180 (Adult), 400 (Credit), 129 (Diabetes), 400 (Nursery), 209 (Malware); VFULR uses a single update (1 epoch) but has the worst fidelity.
Communication cost per epoch is nearly identical across methods; asynchronous unlearning with 25% clients offline per epoch incurs ~25% lower communication cost per epoch than synchronous.

Asynchronous online-rate ablation on Adult Income (LR, 50 unlearning rounds, Table 7): Test Accuracy rises from 0.813 (3 online) to 0.847 (12) / 0.846 (16 online); AUC 0.892 -> 0.896 — number of online clients has minimal effect on fidelity.

Certified-unlearning guarantee (Theorem 1): (epsilon, delta)-certified with delta = 1.5 * exp(-c^2/2), via Gaussian training noise p ~ N(0, c(1+tau*gamma_z*n)*gamma_z*M*|Z|/epsilon)^d plus first-round gradient ascent on removed data.
baselines_compared: VFULR [8] — VFL logistic-regression client removal; single-update method (LR only)
VFUFR [26] — model-agnostic VFL client removal (state-of-the-art baseline); stores original bottom models to accelerate retraining
Retrain — full retraining from scratch on the reduced dataset; treated as the fidelity upper bound and efficiency lower bound
Note: VFULR and VFUFR support only synchronous client removal, so feature/sensitive-info-removal experiments are benchmarked against Retrain only
